sentences.text: Talking about Dr.
Schoenberg, right?
Yeah.
Most remarkable man, most maverick, most charismatic.
An ultra orthodox rabbi who-- what he managed to achieve in post-war Poland, it's incredible.
But then, he was so charismatic that-- and so incredibly good looking at the same time.
And he had this sort of something Churchillian about him.
He was definitely a man of his time.
He came there at the right time and did his job.
I mean, it was very difficult to qualify otherwise.
Because what he did, nobody else did.
And nobody could say no to him.
When he wanted to bring something over, there was no such a way that he would be refused.
That was the charisma that he had.
And he used it.
He used it magnificently.
He knew about it.
But he was primarily interested-- his prime interest was to save the life.
He wanted to bring these children out of there and give them all a chance in life.
Otherwise, who knows?
You cannot imagine the kind of material that he brought over.
You cannot visualize.
Some were bandits.
Some were knife-- knife in every pocket, because it was self-preservation.
They survived the war in woods, among God knows what.
You know.
I mean, they were analphabets.
They couldn't read.
They couldn't write.
They were-- they know on thing, I have to live.
And by all-- any means.
And this man brought this-- some of those people over, who today are prominent people in the world.
Prominence.
This is not to be believed, what they were and what they are.
And if they had stayed behind, who knows what would have become of them?
Criminals, possibly criminals.
Of many.
Because this was-- this is the way they survived the war.
So I was put on the list, on Dr.
Schoenberg's list.
And your mother?
No.
It was only children.
No.
It was terrible for me, again.
I mean, at first, I hear I'm going to London.
Oh, wonderful!
London.
I mean, you know.
My father before the war used to always tell me, oh, wait.
If you're a good girl, and if you study well at school, and if you bring me good marks and so on, you'll go to London to study in England.
And then you'll go maybe to an English school.
You know, he used to tell me these things.
It was like in a book.
Suddenly, I'm going to London.
I didn't do anything about it.
How?
How?
Who?
What?
I do nothing.
I only knew on such and such a date I have to present myself here and there, and then on the boat, onto a boat, and then to London.
And it was really like magic, pure magic.
But, of course, I didn't want to leave my mother.
So I said, no, I'm not going to London without you and so forth.
And my mother said, no, you must go.
Because this is such an opportunity, one in a lifetime.
And you must not reject this.
And people have been trying for, and so on.
You must go.
And I shall see how I can get out there after you.
And so there it was.
We went.
I was in the first transport, in the first group.
And we arrived on in my birthday.
On my birthday, such a present, 1946.
The Tower Bridge opened.
And into Tilbury we docked.
Well, of course, it was all magical.
I mean, there were photographers from all over, journalists from all over.
It was the first group of its kind after the war that came.
So it was all very exotic, I imagine, and-- And we were all dispersed into various families who produced affidavits for us and who guaranteed for us and so on.
And very soon afterwards, I was in a very fortunate position-- really, it was a privileged position-- that I was put to school, not sent to work, like very many have to.
And I was-- Dr.
Schoenberg sent me to school.
I was in one of his schools at the Hasmonean grammar school.
And, of course, I'm eternally grateful to him.
I mean, to me, he's a hero of all heroes, this man.
It's absolutely incredible.
And it's now 50 years--
And now it was 50 years since, and we couldn't celebrate in a sense, because he isn't here with us anymore.
But we did commemorate, and we have not forgotten.
We have thought about it.
We're grateful to him.
And an article appeared in 'The Jewish Chronicle' thanks to our slight endeavours in this direction, because we really fought for it.
Because we thought that this man deserves not an article.
He deserves books to be written about him.
More than books, films to be made about him.
Because what he did to us was heroic deeds, heroic deeds.
He came to Poland.
A little bomb would explode here, there.
They wanted to do away with him.
I don't know where things were happening, amazing things.
Yet he did it.
He had a visa for 60 children.
He brought over 130.
I mean, he did things maverick.
He was a scarlet pimpernel.
He really was.
And on top of that, he was an ultra orthodox rabbi.
Which almost doesn't go together.
It is difficult to conceive.
And if you had seen the way we did, in his uniform, and then saw him in his tallit, it wasn't the same man.
His uniform was conceived by him.
It was-- nothing was-- he had such incredible imagination and drive to do good and to bring and to do-- take these children out.
And he did.
And he did.
He's quite remarkable.
I came here, and I was at this school.
Now about-- within about a month after my arrival, maybe five weeks at school, I'm now asked to the headmaster.
And I thought, oh my god, what have I done now or something.
And the headmaster, of course, our English is very limited still.
And there is a policeman standing.
And I thought, oh god, they're going to deport me or something.
But immediately, I thought I haven't come illegally.
No, they can't do that.
All right.
So the headmaster says, lady, you will now go with this officer to the aliens' department home office, because somebody wants to talk to you there.
Well, he took me into the car and off we went.
I had no idea why, what.
And what have I done, and what are they going to do with me?
I walk into a-- into a very large room.
A very imposing man sitting behind the desk in a uniform, I think, if I remember correctly.
A police uniform.
And he asks me to sit down.
Are you Lilit Stern?
Yes, I'm Lilit Stern.
Hm.
When did you come to this country?
I came on the 29th of March, 1946.
Hm.
Where did you come from?
And starts asking barrage of questions.
Which I know he knows every ounce of them.
He doesn't need to ask me.
He knows it all.
But suddenly, I sort of look down.
His desk is almost empty.
But in front of him, there is a letter.
And I see the handwriting upside down.
The one and only unique handwriting of its kind is that of Mrs.
Wieth, Frau Wieth.
When I saw that handwriting, I knew at that moment why I was here.
Namely, when we were in Krakow, I was desperately trying to find this lady, this Frau Wieth.
What happened to her?
Is she alive?
I mean, she saved our lives.
Where is she?
And I knew addresses in Germany, but I had no reply from there at all.
I used to write letters for her.
She used to call me [NON-ENGLISH].
She used to call me my little-- well, today, you wouldn't say it.
My little Negro Boy.
In other words, my little slave.
Because I used to do everything for her.
And I taught myself, I told you, to read, to write Gothic.
And I wrote so nicely, apparently, like in a book.
I copied the book.
So she was very lazy.
And she said, oh, you can write the letter.
I dictate.
You write to my mother.
You write to my aunts.
And until today, I know all the addresses in my head.
Because I was sitting and writing.
You write a recipe for me.
I had a whole book of recipes written by me.
She had a handwriting which was almost illegible.
Very big, like she was-- and very sort of strong and big, but illegible.
And said, nobody can read my writing.
You write.
You write so nicely.
I was writing.
And I intended to find her.
And so I was writing to all the addresses that I remembered, but I had no replies.
And then, people were going for black marketing a lot-- to Germany, to the newly occupied territories when the war was over-- '45, '46.
They were going to Czechoslovakia, to Germany, all over the place, smuggling and black marketing.
And I one day said to my mother, you know something?
I'm going to-- whoever I know is going-- I'll write a letter and show him I have one photograph of her-- show the photo.
Maybe he'll see her somewhere.
I mean, it's such a stupid thing.
But who knows?
Maybe he will find her.
Maybe he will see her.
And so I was sending such notes, letters.
And if you do, please give her this.
Just before going to England, I went to say goodbye to some friends.
And while there, a man comes in who I knew through these friends of mine.
And he comes.
He says, oh, you say goodbye?
I'm also saying goodbye, because I'm leaving.
I'm going to Germany tomorrow.
I'm going first to Czechoslovakia and then on business.
And I said, are you really?
Oh, listen.
What if I were to give you a little letter?
Who knows, maybe you will come across this lady.
This is my last chance.
I shall never be able to do that when I am in London.
Here is what she looks like.
Look at her closely.
And here is the letter.
Yeah, yeah, sure, I take it.
He took it.
And that letter got to her somehow, found her.
That last letter which this man took when I was-- and in that letter, I said to her, if this doesn't find you, I don't know how, if ever, because I am leaving for London on such and such a date.
I don't know where I shall be in London.
I don't know what will become of me, but I do hope that you are somewhere alive.
That's all.
And she got this letter.
And she wrote to the home office that the girl by the name of so and so should have arrived in London approximately then and then from Poland.
She is looking for this girl.
And that's why they brought me to this.
And I told him, I see-- I see a letter here.
I know this writing.
He said, do you?
Who is this lady to you?
I said, well, she saved my life during the war.
Oh?
Did she now?
And what did you do during the war that she saved your life?
I said, I didn't do anything.
You know, I mean, she took me into hiding.
Why did she take you into hiding?
You cannot imagine the interrogation, because she was German.
Now a letter comes from a German person to me.
Whatever she wrote, I forget-- how happy she is, that she's in a camp somewhere or other.
She would love to come to be with me, to come to England.
And who is this?
A German?
It was immediately after the war.
It was a very precarious thing.
And here I am saying, she saved me.
She had me in hiding.
You know, it was all sort of very suspicious to this officer in those days.
But I eventually managed to explain to him how and what.
And he gave me the letter.
And he said, well, good luck.
I hope she can come one day and so forth.
And, of course, from then on, I found her.
I was in touch with her.
Indeed, within a year-- I'll be now brief, because-- within a year of my arrival-- not exactly a year, a year and two months of my arrival-- we managed to bring my mother over.
Dr.
Schoenberg was extremely helpful in that my school helped me, many people helped me, but they actually brought her over as a domestic to this country.
And she arrived here in May '47.
She had five pounds in her pocket.
I was not allowed to go and meet her at the station, because it was Sabbath, and they wouldn't let me go.
And it was absolutely terrible, because she came to a totally strange foreign country without a word of English, nothing.
Here am I, and I'm not waiting for her at the station.
They wouldn't allow me.
They locked me in, they wouldn't let me go.
And she was waiting and waiting and waiting.
You can imagine how I feel-- or how I felt, rather.
Excuse me.
She was waiting for quite some time.
And as I wasn't coming, she didn't know what happened.
She thought maybe I had an accident, god knows.
So the five pounds, which she was allowed to take out of Poland and bring into here, she hired a taxi.
She brought a sewing machine and five pounds.
She hired a taxi, which cost her 3 pounds 50, and she was left with 1 pound 50 in her pocket when she arrived here and came to my school.
That was all right, for her to come by taxi, on a Sabbath, to school.
That was all right.
But for me to go and fetch her was not all right.
You can see the hypocrisy.
Anyway, so I rebelled a lot about this type of hypocrisy.
Dr.
Schoenberg didn't have it easy with me.
But I think, in his maverick way, he understood me.
He understood, and he was always wonderful to me.
My mother came then.
Within two years, approximately, we brought Mrs.
Wieth over.
She was very unhappy, because she had to work here, and of course, people treated her abominably, as she was a German.
Nobody knew what she did and so on.
No, she never said a word to anyone.
But she was very badly treated, and she didn't want.
She wanted, rather, to go to America.
So with help-- with the help of some people and so forth, we managed that she got an affidavit, and she went to America, and she lived in America very happily.
She was working there.
She was working for a wonderful Jewish family, to whom she was devoted.
Devoted like to the end of their days.
And when they died, she came back here.
And she always wanted to live in England.
Always.
Before the war, she spoke English, French, and German.
She was trilingual, highly intelligent person.
And she wanted to live in England.
That was always her dream.
And so when these people died, she came, and she lived here.
First of all, she was with me, and then she wanted to be by the sea, so she lived in Worthing.
And this is where she died about 10 years ago.
That's Mrs.
Wieth.
A remarkable person, if ever there was one.
She was awarded the highest honor by Israel, of course.
Although she didn't deem it right, because she did not think that she did the slightest heroic deed, thinking-- saying that she's actually a coward.
She is not a hero at all.
It so happened that this situation arose, and this is all that happened.
But it was not because she did anything extraordinary.
Well, this is as it may be.
I quite agree.
She was not a very heroic person.
But what she did, and how we survived, and what she underwent with us was much more than a hero sometimes would.
So she deserved every accolade that there was to-- there was that.
That was that.
Well, I was at school.
When I finished school-- I studied.
I studied here in London.
After my studies, I went to Israel.
In Israel, I went with an international Jewish student organization-- inter-university student organization to work for six weeks.
Work and have some activities to learn, to see the country.
But I stayed for certainly longer than six weeks.
I stayed for six months.
I came to give regards to a gentleman, to an elderly gentleman, through friend of mine who asked me to pass regards to him.
And he introduced me to his son.
And his son, as it is, became very, very interested and so persuaded me to stay longer.
And so I stayed for six months.
And he was in the Israeli forces at the time, professionally.
He was an officer in the army.
And I said, listen.
I cannot possibly be an army wife, not in those days.
It's out of the question.
My nerves are too shot for that.
I couldn't possibly-- when you don't get back and so, I can't think, what happened?
I can't do that.
That's not-- I'm not cut out for it.
So I'm terribly sorry, but I'm going back to London.
And that was it.
I went back to London.
But after awhile, he decided he wants to come and study here and so forth, which he did.
And we were married here.
Dr.
Schoenberg married us.
His name?
Yes.
[NON-ENGLISH].
That was his name.
And he's also from Lwow.
Chances are he would do what I'm doing in America, because he lives in Florida now.
And we-- he wanted to go to America.
We went eventually to America.
He went first, I followed.
After I was married, my mother married, too, remarried, after many, many years of being alone.
She was a very attractive, very lovely lady, but she just couldn't conceive-- my father and she were such a marriage made in heaven.
And it was just not on.
But after all these years, yes.
And so she remarried.
So I was-- I went to America.
It was a slightly less burden in my heart knowing that she's looked after, and very nice man she married also.
Very nice, and everything was fine and lovely.
And then, very soon afterwards, I had my daughter, Karen, in America.
And we lived, and everything was fine.
I worked partially, yes.
Not to begin with.
I worked immediately when I came to America, because I was-- I was recommended from London, from my firm in London to their Wall Street offices.
Yes.
Mhm.
But very soon afterwards-- and I was pregnant, so I really couldn't take these journeys to the-- it was impossible.
The American subway, and me and my condition, I could not.
And I had to give it up.
So for awhile, I did not.
But then, when Karen was about 2 and 1/2, 3 years, I was offered a very interesting position to observe at the United Nations for Monaco.
And that was lovely.
We ran also at the same time.
It was a travel office and not an agency.
But the state travel office for information and so on.
And the observer to the United Nations.
And so I worked part-time, because the child was small.
But it was very interesting for me to do that at the Rockefeller Center and in New York.
And it was at the time when Khrushchev was banging his-- and the war was almost imminent.
And they were interesting times.
And in 1963, I came back to London with Karen.
Because my husband and I, unfortunately, we were divorced.
Which is very sad, but so it happened.
And we are friendly.
We were good friends and so forth.
But unfortunately not as husband and wife.
And he stayed in America, and I came back here.
I working here and so on.
And then years later, I was working constantly.
Karen was going to school.
She was at Saint Paul's here, and then at Godolphin and [INAUDIBLE].
And this is why I moved to this part of the world.
Because we used to live in Hampstead, but I wanted her within a walking distance to school.
And that was good.
And afterwards, she went back to America-- to study there, to work there and so on, and came back afterwards.
And I was remarried in 1974.
Sorry, in 1975.
Unfortunately, did not last very, very long.
My husband-- my then husband, late husband, was an actor.
And his name was Eric Pohlmann, a rather well-known actor.
People here will know-- know him well.
And a most delightful man.
Absolutely kind, wonderful, clever, wise, fun.
Everything in one person.
And it was very nice life, very interesting life.
I mean, he played a lot abroad.
And we were a lot abroad while he was playing at theatre, and television, films, and so on.
And he died very, very suddenly, from one second to the next.
He was holding his inevitable cigarette in one hand and his inevitable cup of coffee in the other.
And mid-sentence, keeled over and had a very massive heart attack.
And that was in Salzburg, just before the festival opened.
He was due to appear at this festival.
And there was a banquet, opening banquet that night.
This happened in the morning, on the 25th of July, 1979.
